analogy: Think of "Plant Kingdom" through the chapter's own opening images: PLANT KINGDOM 23 In the previous chapter, we looked at the broad classification of living organisms under the system proposed by Whittaker (1969) wherein he suggested the Five Kingdom classification viz. Monera, Protista, Fungi, Animalia and Plantae. In this chapter, we will deal in detail... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: PLANT KINGDOM 23, PLANT KINGDOM, CHAPTER 3, 3.1 Algae. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Plant Kingdom is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that pLANT KINGDOM 23 In the previous chapter, we looked at the broad classification of living organisms under the system proposed by Whittaker (1969) wherein he suggested the Five Kingdom classification viz.

The text explains that monera, Protista, Fungi, Animalia and Plantae.

The text explains that in this chapter, we will deal in detail with further classification within Kingdom Plantae popularly known as the ‘plant kingdom’.

The text explains that we must stress here that our understanding of the plant kingdom has changed over time.

The text explains that fungi, and members of the Monera and Protista having cell walls have now been excluded from Plantae though earlier classifications placed them in the same kingdom.

The text explains that so, the cyanobacteria that are also referred to as blue green algae are not ‘algae’ any more.

The text explains that in this chapter, we will describe A lgae, Bryophytes, Pteridophytes, Gymnosperms and A ngiosperms under Plantae.

The text explains that let us also look at classification within angiosperms to understand some of the concerns that influenced the classification systems.

The text explains that the earliest systems of classification used only gross superficial morphological characters such as habit, colour, number and shape of leaves, etc.

The text explains that they were based mainly on vegetative characters or on the androecium structure (system given by Linnaeus).

The text explains that such systems were artificial; they separated the closely related species since they were based on a few characteristics.

The text explains that also, the artificial systems gave equal weightage to vegetative and sexual characteristics; this is not acceptable since we know that often the vegetative characters are more easily affected by environment.

The text explains that as against this, natural classification systems developed, which were based on natural affinities among the organisms and consider, PLANT KINGDOM CHAPTER 3 3.1 Algae 3.2 Bryophytes 3.3 Pteridophytes 3.4 Gymnosperms 3.5 Angiosperms

The text explains that 24 BIOLOGY not only the external features, but also internal features, like ultra- structure, anatomy, embryology and phytochemistry.

The text explains that such a classification for flowering plants was given by George Bentham and Joseph Dalton Hooker.

The text explains that at present phylogenetic classification systems based on evolutionary relationships between the various organisms are acceptable.

The text explains that we now use information from many other sources too to help resolve difficulties in classification.

The text explains that number and codes are assigned to all the characters and the data are then processed.

The text explains that cytotaxonomy, that is based on cytological information like chromosome number, structure, behaviour and chemotaxonomy, that uses the chemical constituents of the plant to resolve confusions, are also used by taxonomists these days.

The text explains that at least a half of the total carbon dioxide fixation on earth is carried out by algae through photosynthesis.

The text explains that the food is stored as floridean starch which is very similar to amylopectin and glycogen in structure.

The text explains that they play an important role in plant succession on bare rocks/soil.

The text explains that they may possess root-like, leaf-like or stem-like structures.

The text explains that mosses along with lichens are the first organisms to colonise rocks and hence, are of great ecological importance.

The text explains that they decompose rocks making the substrate suitable for the growth of higher plants.

The text explains that the leafy members have tiny leaf-like appendages in two rows on the stem-like structures.

The text explains that asexual reproduction in liverworts takes place by fragmentation of thalli, or by the formation of specialised structures called gemmae (sing. gemma).

The text explains that the first stage is the protonema stage, which develops directly from a spore.

The text explains that they consist of upright, slender axes bearing spirally arranged leaves.

The text explains that you shall study more about these tissues in Chapter 6.

The text explains that in some cases sporophylls may form distinct compact structures called strobili or cones (Selaginella, Equisetum).

The text explains that the leaves in gymnosperms are well-adapted to withstand extremes of temperature, humidity and wind.

The text explains that the nucellus is protected by envelopes and the composite structure is called an ovule.

The text explains that 34 BIOLOGY 3.5 ANGIOSPERMS Unlike the gymnosperms where the ovules are naked, in the angiosperms or flowering plants, the pollen grains and ovules are developed in specialised structures called flowers.

The text explains that the plant body of liverworts is thalloid and dorsiventral whereas mosses have upright, slender axes bearing spirally arranged leaves.

The text explains that explain briefly the following terms with suitable examples:- (i) protonema (ii) antheridium (iii) archegonium (iv) diplontic (v) sporophyll (vi) isogamy 9.

The text explains that when and where does reduction division take place in the life cycle of a liverwort, a moss, a fern, a gymnosperm and an angiosperm?

The text explains that name three groups of plants that bear archegonia.

The text explains that briefly describe the life cycle of any one of them.

The text explains that mention the ploidy of the following: protonemal cell of a moss; primary endosperm nucleus in dicot, leaf cell of a moss; prothallus cell of a ferm; gemma cell in Marchantia; meristem cell of monocot, ovum of a liverwort, and zygote of a fern.

The text explains that write a note on economic importance of algae and gymnosperms.

The text explains that both gymnosperms and angiosperms bear seeds, then why are they classified separately?

The text explains that differentiate between the following:- (i) red algae and brown algae (ii) liverworts and moss (iii) homosporous and heterosporous pteridophyte 10.

The text explains that match the following (column I with column II) Column I Column II (a) Chlamydomonas (i) Moss (b) Cycas (ii) Pteridophyte (c) Selaginella (iii) Algae (d) Sphagnum (iv) Gymnosperm 11.

The text explains that describe the important characteristics of gymnosperms.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Biology, Chapter 3: Plant Kingdom
